title: Saving notes isn't the same as remembering them
Knowledge grows when notes connect, not just pile up
Knowledge compounds when it's linked, not just stored
Knowledge compounds through links, not storage
Linked knowledge compounds; stored knowledge doesn't
storage-isnt-recall: Saving something feels like you got work done. But a folder full of saved clippings doesn't really do anything by itself. You could save a thousand bits of text you liked and still sit down to a blank page with no idea where to start. That's because keeping a note is not the same as remembering it. The notes just sit there, on their own, not joined to anything, and not one of them shows you where to go next. Which is worth noticing: the good part may be less about having the note saved away than about being able to find it again at the moment a new idea needs it.

Saving things feels like progress, but a folder of clippings doesn't do much on its own. You can save a thousand highlights and still face a blank page, because keeping a note isn't the same as remembering it — the notes just sit there, unconnected, and none of them points you anywhere. Which is worth noticing: the value may lie less in having the idea filed away than in being able to reach it when a different idea needs it.

Saving things feels like progress, but a folder of clippings does nothing on its own. You can hoard a thousand highlights and still arrive at a blank page, because storage isn't recall — the notes sit there, unconnected, and none of them points you anywhere. Which is worth weighing: the value may lie less in having the idea filed than in being able to reach it when a different idea needs it.

Saving feels like progress, but a folder of clippings does nothing alone. Hoard a thousand highlights and you still arrive at a blank page: storage isn't recall. The notes sit unconnected, pointing nowhere. Worth weighing: the value may lie less in filing the idea than in reaching it when another idea needs it.
Saving isn't progress; storage isn't recall. Unconnected notes point nowhere. Worth weighing: value may lie less in filing an idea than in reaching it on demand.
links-make-a-web: Links are the thing that turns a heap of notes into a web. When one note points to another note, every new link you add makes both of those notes easier to find later, and the whole collection becomes more useful much faster than it grows in size. That's the reason Evergreen notes are written to evolve, not to file — they're made to be linked together — and it's why Structure beats freeform text for working with AI when you want to really do something with what you saved. Even one single page can hold these threads for you, as A page can remember what you learn.

Links are what turn a pile of notes into a web. When one note names another, each new connection makes both ends easier to find, and the whole collection grows in usefulness faster than it grows in size. That's why Evergreen notes are written to evolve, not to file — they're written to be linked — and why Structure beats freeform text for working with AI when you want to actually work with what you saved. Even a single page can hold the threads for you, as A page can remember what you learn.

That's why Evergreen notes are written to evolve, not to file — they're written to be linked — and why Structure beats freeform text for working with AI when you want to actually work with what you saved. Hence Evergreen notes are written to evolve, not to file — built to be linked — and why Structure beats freeform text for working with AI when you want to work with what you saved. Links make a web; each one boosts both ends' findability, so usefulness outpaces size. Thus Evergreen notes are written to evolve, not to file and Structure beats freeform text for working with AI. One page suffices: A page can remember what you learn.
rag-needs-links: This is also how AI that looks things up for you starts to pull its weight. That kind of AI doesn't make knowledge out of thin air. Instead, it goes and finds the things you already linked together, so the better your connections are, the better the answers it brings back will be. Have a look at the frameworks for memory systems.

This is also how retrieval-augmented AI starts to pull its weight. It doesn't make knowledge from nothing; it fetches what you already connected, so the better your connections, the better what comes back. See the frameworks for memory systems.

This is also how retrieval-augmented AI earns its keep. It doesn't conjure knowledge; it retrieves what you already connected, and the better your connections, the better what comes back. See the frameworks for memory systems.
Retrieval-augmented AI earns its keep the same way. It doesn't conjure knowledge; it retrieves what you connected, and better connections yield better results. See the frameworks for memory systems.
Retrieval-augmented AI retrieves, not conjures: better connections, better output. See the frameworks for memory systems.
